content:
The **multilayer perceptron** idea grew from a simple question: how do you learn decision rules **more complex than a straight cut** in input space?

**Rosenblatt's perceptron (1957)** showed that a single threshold unit could learn linearly separable patterns with a simple update rule. Enthusiasm cooled when **Minsky and Papert (1969)** proved limitations — notably that a single-layer perceptron **cannot represent XOR** — which damped funding for neural-network research through much of the 1970s.

Researchers still knew that **adding hidden units** could, in principle, build richer functions. The missing piece was a **scalable training procedure** for those hidden weights. **Rumelhart, Hinton, and Williams (1986)** popularized **error backpropagation** for multilayer networks, showing how to propagate loss gradients through hidden layers and making MLP training practical.

From the 1990s onward, MLPs became the default **feedforward classifier** for tabular and small-vector tasks. Convolutional networks later dominated images and Transformers dominated sequences, but the same **hidden-layer + activation** pattern persists inside their **feed-forward sublayers** — the MLP remains the atomic nonlinear stack of modern deep learning.

timeline.label: 1957 — Perceptron
timeline.description: Rosenblatt introduces the trainable threshold neuron — one layer, linearly separable problems.

timeline.label: 1969 — Linear limits
timeline.description: Minsky & Papert highlight XOR and related failures of single-layer perceptrons, slowing neural-net research.

timeline.label: 1986 — Backpropagation
timeline.description: Rumelhart, Hinton & Williams demonstrate practical gradient-based training of networks with hidden layers.

timeline.label: 2010s–present — MLP inside larger models
timeline.description: Dense MLP blocks remain standard in Transformer FFN layers and as baselines for tabular and embedding tasks.
